title: Reading the Impedance Plane
narrationScript: Every eddy current signal you will ever interpret lives on one picture: the impedance plane. Resistance runs along the horizontal axis, inductive reactance up the vertical, and your probe is a single bright dot. Each test variable pushes that dot in its own direction, and that direction is the information. Lift-off drags the dot toward the air point and is the biggest, fastest mover, so we rotate the display until it runs horizontal. Conductivity slides the operating point along the comma-shaped curve — titanium near the top, copper near the bottom — and raising frequency walks the point down it exactly as if conductivity had increased. A surface crack interrupts the currents and drives a sharp signal upward, well separated from lift-off. Deeper flaws answer later and weaker: phase lag rotates their signals about fifty-seven degrees per standard depth of penetration while amplitude shrinks. Permeability swings the dot harder than any flaw, which is why ferromagnetic parts get magnetically saturated. Read direction first, then amplitude: phase tells you what it is and how deep — amplitude tells you how much.

sections.heading: The Plane Where Every Signal Lives
sections.body:
Pull together what the last three lessons built. Your coil carries alternating current, so it owns a **primary magnetic field**. Hold that field against a conductor and it induces **eddy currents**; the eddy currents build a **secondary field** that opposes the primary; and that opposition arrives back at the coil terminals as a change in **impedance**. Impedance has exactly two parts — **resistance R** and **inductive reactance XL = 2πfL** — so everything that happens in the part reaches you as some combination of two numbers.

Two numbers define a point. Plot **R on the horizontal axis** and **XL on the vertical axis** and you have the **impedance plane**: a map on which the probe's condition at any instant is a single dot. The distance from the origin to the dot is the impedance magnitude **Z = √(R² + XL²)**; the angle it makes with the horizontal is the **phase angle θ**.

Couple the probe to a conductor and watch the dot move. Eddy-current losses pull energy from the coil, so **R rises**; the opposing secondary field cancels part of the flux, so **XL falls** below its air value. Every test variable — spacing, conductivity, a crack — pushes the dot in **its own characteristic direction**. Older meter-style instruments displayed only the magnitude as a needle swing, so a crack and a probe wobble could look identical. The modern **phase-analysis display** lets you **null** (balance) the dot at a chosen screen position and read both the **size and the direction** of every excursion. Direction is the new information — and this lesson is about learning those directions.

sections.caption: The normalized impedance plane: the comma-shaped conductivity curve runs from the air point down through titanium, stainless, aluminum, and copper; lift-off paths return to the air point; ferromagnetic material climbs above it.

sections.heading: The Conductivity Curve: One Map for Every Metal
sections.body:
Raw R and XL values depend on the particular coil, so instruments and textbooks **normalize** the plane: subtract the coil's own wire resistance, then divide both axes by the empty-coil reactance **X0**. On the normalized plane every probe tells the same story. With the probe in air, the dot rests at the **air point** — normalized reactance 1.0, resistance 0.

Touch the probe to metal and the dot lands somewhere on the **conductivity curve**, the comma-shaped locus that organizes every nonmagnetic conductor:

- **Low-conductivity metals** sit near the top: titanium alloys around **1% IACS**, austenitic stainless steels around **2–3% IACS**.
- **Aluminum alloys** occupy the middle-to-lower region — roughly **25–60% IACS** depending on alloy and temper.
- **Copper** — **100% IACS** by definition of the International Annealed Copper Standard — sits far down the curve.

Now the powerful part: the operating point's position depends on the **product of conductivity and frequency** (together with probe and part geometry). Raising the test frequency walks the dot down and around the curve exactly as if the metal had become more conductive. You cannot change the alloy — but you choose the frequency, which means **you choose where on the map you operate**. Why it matters: the angle between the lift-off direction and the conductivity curve **widens** as the point moves down the map, and separating variables from each other is the entire game of signal interpretation.

sections.heading: Lift-Off: Nuisance, Tool, and Reference Direction
sections.body:
**Lift-off** is the change in electromagnetic coupling as the gap between probe and surface changes. On the map it has one fixed habit: from wherever the operating point sits, increasing lift-off drives the dot along a nearly straight path **back to the air point** — at large spacing, the part might as well not exist.

Three facts make lift-off the first signal you must master:

- **It is the biggest, fastest mover.** Coupling dies off steeply with distance, so the **first few thousandths of an inch** of gap produce the largest excursion. Probe wobble, paint steps, and surface roughness all speak in the lift-off direction.
- **It is a nuisance** on crack searches — wobble noise can bury a flaw signal — which is why probes are spring-loaded, held square to the surface, and scanned with discipline.
- **It is a tool** when spacing is exactly what you want to measure: calibrate the lift-off response and you have a **nonconductive coating-thickness gauge** for paint or anodic films over metal.

Because lift-off can never be eliminated, instruments give you a **phase rotation** control. The standard field convention: rotate the display until the lift-off trace runs **horizontally** — commonly moving left as the probe lifts. Conductivity meters, typically operating near **60 kHz** on aluminum alloys, exploit the same geometry: they work at an operating point where lift-off and conductivity separate cleanly, so small probe-spacing variations do not corrupt the %-IACS reading.

sections.title: Null, lift, rotate — then trust your directions
sections.body: Set up the display the same way every time. **(1) Null** the dot on a known-good area of the reference standard. **(2) Lift** the probe and watch the trace move. **(3) Rotate** the phase control until that lift-off trace runs horizontally toward the left edge of the screen. **(4) Scan the reference notches** and set gain so the crack signal rises well up the screen. From that moment, direction has meaning: horizontal motion is spacing, a steep upward excursion is a surface crack, and a rotated, shrunken version of the crack signal is a flaw buried deeper.

sections.caption: Calibrated screen with lift-off rotated horizontal: spacing changes run left toward the air point, a surface crack rises steeply, and a subsurface flaw appears rotated clockwise and smaller because of phase lag.

sections.heading: Phase Tells Depth, Amplitude Tells How Much
sections.body:
A crack is a wall across the current's path. Eddy currents flow in closed loops parallel to the coil winding; a surface crack forces them to **divert around and beneath it**, which both lowers the effective conductivity the coil sees and disturbs the coupling. The result is a sharp signal at a **distinct angle** from lift-off — with the standard rotation, the trace rises steeply as the coil crosses the crack and returns as it leaves. **Amplitude** scales with how much current the flaw interrupts: longer, deeper cracks interrupt more and swing harder.

Depth is written in the **phase**. Current flowing deeper in the part **lags** the surface current by **β = x/δ radians — about 57° per standard depth of penetration** — while its density decays to **37% at 1δ** and about **14% at 2δ**. So a subsurface flaw answers later and weaker: its signal is **rotated** away from the surface-crack direction (clockwise on the common setup) and **smaller** for the same metal loss. That rotation is your depth gauge — in heat-exchanger tube testing, the phase angle of a signal is read against a calibration curve to report through-wall depth. The practical limit: beyond the **effective depth of penetration, about 3δ**, only ~5% of the current density remains — too little to interrogate anything, so frequency must be chosen for the depth you need.

One more mover you must recognize on sight: **ferromagnetic material**. Permeability multiplies the coil's inductance, swinging the dot hard in the **reactance direction** — above the air point on the map — and ordinary permeability variations from heat treatment or cold work produce signals that **dwarf flaw responses**. The fix is **magnetic saturation**: a strong DC field drives the relative permeability toward 1 so the steel behaves like a nonmagnetic conductor while it is tested.

sections.caption: Phase lag through the thickness: current density falls to 37% at one standard depth of penetration and lags 57°, so the same flaw moved one δ deeper produces a rotated, weaker screen signal.
Impedance-plane response quick card — display convention: lift-off rotated horizontal, pointing left.
rows: Lift-off / probe wobble
Horizontal, toward the air point
Large and fast; repeats with every probe lift; first few mils of gap move it most
Conductivity change
Along the conductivity curve
Slow drift between alloys, tempers, or heat-treat conditions — not a sharp flick
Surface crack
Rises steeply, well separated from lift-off
Sharp excursion and return as the coil crosses; grows with crack depth and length
Subsurface flaw
Rotated farther by phase lag (≈57° per δ of depth)
Smaller than a surface flaw of the same size; rotation increases with depth
Permeability (ferromagnetic part)
Strongly in the reactance direction, above the air point
Very large and erratic; suppress with magnetic saturation
Wall thinning
Along its own thickness locus, distinct from lift-off
Gradual; visible only while the wall is within roughly 3δ
Edge effect
Large, repeatable sweep as the probe nears an edge
Predictable from geometry — keep a fixed scan path or use a shielded probe

figures.title: The normalized impedance plane and conductivity curve
Screen signatures with lift-off rotated horizontal
Phase lag: depth rotates and shrinks the signal

interactive.title: Which Trace Is the Probe Wobble?
interactive.intro: Same setup as the recognition card: aluminum skin, nulled on clean bare metal, lift-off rotated horizontal. Halfway down the scan your wrist rocks and the probe tilts slightly off the surface — twice. Click the trace that motion produces.

regions.label: Horizontal trace running left
regions.feedback: Correct. A wobble is a spacing change, and every spacing change speaks in the lift-off direction — horizontal on this setup, headed for the air point. That is exactly why we rotate lift-off flat: rocking probes and paint steps stay horizontal while cracks break upward.

regions.label: Bright dot
regions.feedback: That is the null point — the balanced resting position you parked the dot at on clean metal. It is where signals start from, not a signal itself.

regions.label: Steep upward lobe
regions.feedback: This steep excursion is the surface-crack direction, deliberately separated in phase from lift-off. A rocking wrist cannot draw this trace — that separation is the entire reason the display is set up this way.

regions.label: Rotated, smaller lobe
regions.feedback: This lobe is rotated clockwise from the surface-crack direction and smaller — the signature of a flaw below the surface answering with phase lag, not a spacing change. Wobble lives on the horizontal lift-off line.

checkQuestions.question: On a standard impedance-plane display, the two axes represent:
options: Coil voltage and coil current
Resistance (horizontal) and inductive reactance (vertical)
Test frequency and signal amplitude
Conductivity and permeability
checkQuestions.explanation: Coil impedance has exactly two components — resistance R and inductive reactance XL = 2πfL. Plotting R horizontally and XL vertically turns the probe's electrical state into a single dot whose excursions carry both magnitude and phase information.

checkQuestions.question: While scanning an aluminum part, the probe lifts slightly off the surface. On the normalized impedance plane, the operating point moves:
options: Down the conductivity curve toward the copper position
Vertically upward, far above the air point
Along the lift-off path toward the air point
Nowhere — spacing changes do not affect the coil
checkQuestions.explanation: Increasing the gap weakens coupling, so the dot retreats along a nearly straight lift-off path that always terminates at the air point — at large spacing the part effectively is not there. Movement above the air point is a permeability effect, not lift-off.

checkQuestions.question: With the same probe on the same nonmagnetic part, raising the test frequency moves the operating point:
options: Back toward the air point
Above the air point, onto the ferromagnetic branch
Nowhere — frequency only changes signal amplitude
Down and around the conductivity curve, the same direction as a conductivity increase
checkQuestions.explanation: The operating point's position depends on the product of conductivity and frequency, so raising f walks the dot down the comma-shaped curve exactly as if conductivity had increased — and the phase separation between lift-off and flaw signals improves lower on the map.

checkQuestions.question: Compared with a surface crack of similar size, the signal from a subsurface flaw is:
options: Rotated by phase lag — about 57° per standard depth of penetration — and smaller in amplitude
Identical, because flaw depth does not change the signal
Larger, because more metal surrounds the flaw
Aligned exactly with the lift-off direction
checkQuestions.explanation: Currents deeper in the part lag the surface current by β = x/δ (≈57° per δ) and their density decays to 37% at 1δ, so deeper flaws produce rotated, weaker signals. That predictable rotation is the basis of phase-angle depth sizing.

checkQuestions.question: Scanning a steel (ferromagnetic) fitting, you see large, erratic signals swinging mostly in the reactance direction across the whole part, masking the reference-notch response. The most likely cause and standard remedy are:
options: Edge effect — increase the scanning speed
Permeability variations — apply magnetic saturation with a strong DC field
Probe wobble — simply hold the probe more steadily
Conductivity drift — recalibrate the conductivity meter
checkQuestions.explanation: Permeability multiplies coil inductance, driving the dot strongly in the reactance direction above the air point, and normal heat-treat or cold-work variations swamp flaw signals. Saturating the part with a DC field flattens relative permeability toward 1 so it tests like a nonmagnetic conductor. Wobble would appear in the lift-off direction, and edge effect appears only near edges.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, ECT Level I Theory Course §2.1.1–2.1.3 (eddy current generation, impedance changes, and instrument response)
ASNT SNT-TC-1A (2024), Eddy Current Testing Method — Level I topical outline
ASTM E1004, Standard Test Method for Determining Electrical Conductivity Using the Electromagnetic (Eddy Current) Method
ASTM E309, Standard Practice for Eddy Current Examination of Steel Tubular Products Using Magnetic Saturation
